---
title: "$M_1 \\to M_2$ Stacking: Aligned Template Integration"
format:
  html:
    html-math-method: mathjax
    self-contained: true
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    page-layout: full
    grid:
      body-width: 1200px
---

```{=html}
<style>
h1 {
  margin-top: 1.8em;
  padding: 0.45em 1em;
  background: #1a3a5c;
  color: #ffffff;
  border-radius: 5px;
}
h2 {
  margin-top: 1.8em;
  padding: 0.4em 0.9em;
  border-left: 5px solid #1e5f8e;
  background: #e8f1f8;
  color: #1a3a5c;
  border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
}
h3 {
  margin-top: 2em;
  padding: 0.4em 0.8em;
  border-left: 4px solid #4a90d9;
  background: #f4f8fd;
  border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
}
h4 {
  margin-top: 1.5em;
  padding: 0.3em 0.7em;
  border-left: 3px solid #a0bfe0;
  background: #f9fbfe;
  border-radius: 0 3px 3px 0;
}
.callout-note {
  border-left: 4px solid #2196F3;
  background: #e3f2fd;
  padding: 1em;
  margin: 1.2em 0;
  border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
}
.callout-warning {
  border-left: 4px solid #FF9800;
  background: #fff3e0;
  padding: 1em;
  margin: 1.2em 0;
  border-radius: 0 4px 4px 0;
}
</style>
```

This document describes how the $M_1$ alignment model's **predictions** replace
$M_2$'s static template backbone, and the implications for training, evaluation,
and deployment. It supersedes the static template $z_{s,w}(\delta, Kr)$ described
in the [pipeline overview](pipeline_overview.html) §6 and
[forecast training](forecast_training.html) §1.

## Motivation: the static-template problem

In the original $M_2$ specification
([pipeline overview](pipeline_overview.html) §6), the template backbone is

$$
z_{s,w}(\delta, Kr) = \omega(t_{s,w};\, Kr)\;\mathrm{logit}_\varepsilon\!\bigl(g_\text{ref}(w^a_{s,w} + \delta)\bigr),
$$

where $\delta$ is a fixed integer-week shift tuned by LOSO, and $g_\text{ref}$
is the reference curve. This has two problems:

1. **$\delta$ is static.** The same shift is used at every week of every season.
   In reality, the optimal alignment changes as data accumulate — $M_1$ exists
   precisely to capture this.

2. **$M_1$'s work is discarded.** $M_1$ fits a 4-parameter dilation model
   $(\tau_w, \delta_w, a_w, b_w)$ that adapts weekly. The static template
   ignores this entirely, using only the coarse $w^a_{s,w}$ alignment from
   ignition anchoring.

The solution is **model stacking**: use $M_1$'s prediction $\hat\pi^{M_1}_{s,w}$
as $M_2$'s template feature instead of the static reference curve.


## Stacking architecture {#sec-stacking}

### Notation

Recall from the [pipeline overview](pipeline_overview.html):

- $g_\text{ref}(u)$: reference curve on the logit scale, defined on aligned
  time $u$
- $M_1$ fits $(\tau_w, \delta_w, a_w, b_w)$ at each evaluation week $w$ and
  produces a predicted positivity curve:

$$
\mathrm{logit}\!\bigl(\hat\pi^{M_1}_{s,w}(t)\bigr) = a_w + b_w \cdot g_\text{ref}\!\!\left(\frac{t - \tau_w}{1 + \delta_w}\right)
$$

for any aligned time point $t$. In particular, $M_1$ can predict at
**future** target weeks $w^a_{s,w+h}$ for $h = 1, 2$.

### New template feature

Define the **stacked template** covariate:

$$
\tilde{z}^h_{s,w} = \mathrm{logit}_\varepsilon\!\Bigl(\hat\pi^{M_1}_{s,w}\!\bigl(w^a_{s,w+h}\bigr)\Bigr),
$$ {#eq-stacked-z}

where $w^a_{s,w+h}$ is the aligned time at the target week $w + h$.
This replaces the static $z_{s,w}(\delta, Kr)$ in $M_2$'s linear predictor.

The key difference is that $\tilde{z}^h_{s,w}$ is computed from $M_1$'s
**time-varying** alignment parameters $(\tau_w, \delta_w, a_w, b_w)$
evaluated at the current week $w$, not from the fixed reference curve with
a static shift $\delta$.

### Updated $M_2$ linear predictor

The $M_2$ model becomes:

$$
\eta^h_{s,w} = \mathrm{logit}\!\bigl(\pi^h_{s,w}\bigr)
= \alpha^h + b_s
+ \underbrace{\tilde{z}^h_{s,w}}_{\text{M}_1\text{ template}}
+ S_2^h\!\bigl(w^a_{s,w}\bigr)
+ S_{3,s}^h\!\bigl(w^a_{s,w}\bigr)
+ S_4^h\!\bigl(z^{(\mathrm{ema})}_{s,w}\bigr)
+ S_6^h\!\bigl(d1_{s,w}\bigr)
+ S_7^h\!\bigl(d2_{s,w}\bigr).
$$ {#eq-stacked-m2}

Compared to the original specification, the only structural change is:

| Original | Stacked |
|---|---|
| $z_{s,w}(\delta, Kr) = \omega(t;\,Kr)\;\mathrm{logit}_\varepsilon\!\bigl(g_\text{ref}(w^a + \delta)\bigr)$ | $\tilde{z}^h_{s,w} = \mathrm{logit}_\varepsilon\!\bigl(\hat\pi^{M_1}_{s,w}(w^a_{s,w+h})\bigr)$ |

**The template entry mode `T` and ramp `Kr` are unchanged** — $\tilde{z}^h_{s,w}$
enters the model exactly as $z_{s,w}$ did (offset, smooth, or excluded).
The hyperparameter $\delta$ (static integer-week shift) is **eliminated** because
$M_1$ already handles alignment timing.

::: {.callout-note}
#### Why pass predictions, not parameters?

One could instead pass $M_1$'s fitted parameters $(\tau_w, \delta_w, a_w, b_w)$
as four separate covariates. This has two disadvantages:

1. **Dimensionality.** Four correlated, time-varying parameters require more
   complex interaction terms. A single logit-scale prediction compresses them
   into the quantity $M_2$ actually needs.

2. **Interpretability.** The stacked prediction $\tilde{z}^h_{s,w}$ is on the
   same logit scale as $M_2$'s response — it is directly comparable to the
   old $z_{s,w}$ and inherits the same model entry mechanisms.

This is standard stacking: stage-1 predictions are features for stage-2.
:::

## Training: walk-forward M1 predictions {#sec-training}

### The leakage constraint

$M_2$'s template feature $\tilde{z}^h_{s,w}$ is itself a model output from
$M_1$. If we used retro-fitted $M_1$ outputs (full-season alignment) as
training features, $M_2$ would overfit to unrealistically good $M_1$ predictions
that are unavailable at forecast time.

**Requirement:** $M_2$ must be trained on $M_1$ predictions that were generated
**prospectively** — using only data available at evaluation week $w$.

### Walk-forward M1 generation

For each training season $s$ and evaluation week $w \ge w_s^{(\mathrm{ign})}$,
we run $M_1$ prospectively:

$$
\hat\pi^{M_1}_{s,w}(\cdot) \;\leftarrow\;
\texttt{run\_alignment\_prospective}\!\bigl(\text{data}_{s,1:w},\; g_\text{ref}^{(-s^*)},\; \text{hyper}^{(-s^*)}\bigr),
$$

where $g_\text{ref}^{(-s^*)}$ denotes the reference curve estimated with the
current LOSO fold's test season $s^*$ excluded.

This produces $M_1$'s forecast curve at week $w$. We then interpolate to
get predictions at target weeks:

$$
\hat{p}^{M_1}_{s,w,h} = \hat\pi^{M_1}_{s,w}\!\bigl(w^a_{s,w+h}\bigr),
\qquad h \in \{1, 2\},
$$

using linear interpolation on the predicted curve
(`stats::approx(forecast_df$newWeek, forecast_df$p_hat, xout = w^a_{s,w+h})`).

::: {.callout-note}
#### Target week computation

The target aligned week is:

$$
w^a_{s,w+h} = (w^f_{s,w} + h) - \hat{w}^{(\mathrm{ign})}_s + w^{an},
$$

where $\hat{w}^{(\mathrm{ign})}_s$ is the detected ignition week and $w^{an}$
is the anchor week (median ignition across training seasons).
:::

### LOSO integration

In each LOSO fold with test season $s^*$:

1. **Reference curve.** Fit $g_\text{ref}^{(-s^*)}$ on all seasons except $s^*$
   (as usual).

2. **$M_1$ walk-forward on training seasons.** For each training season
   $s \ne s^*$, run $M_1$ prospectively week by week. Collect predictions
   $\hat{p}^{M_1}_{s,w,h}$ for all $(s, w, h)$. This uses the same
   $g_\text{ref}^{(-s^*)}$, so the training seasons' M1 predictions are
   generated with the test season excluded from the reference curve.

3. **Build $M_2$ training data.** Stack training rows across seasons and
   horizons. For each row $(s, w, h)$, set:

$$
\texttt{logit\_f\_eff}_{s,w,h} = \mathrm{logit}_\varepsilon\!\bigl(\hat{p}^{M_1}_{s,w,h}\bigr).
$$

4. **$M_1$ walk-forward on test season.** Run $M_1$ on $s^*$ prospectively
   to get $\hat{p}^{M_1}_{s^*,w,h}$.

5. **Score.** Predict $s^*$ as a new season (season-dependent terms excluded)
   using $M_2$ fitted in step 3, and compute NLL over the evaluation window.

```
LOSO fold for test season s*:
  ├─ estimateRef(−s*)  →  g_ref, hyper
  ├─ M0: detect ignition for each season
  ├─ M1 walk-forward on training seasons  →  m1_train_preds
  ├─ train_stage2_joint(m1_preds = m1_train_preds)  →  M2 fit
  ├─ M1 walk-forward on test season s*  →  m1_test_preds
  └─ score M2 on s* using m1_test_preds  →  NLL
```

::: {.callout-warning}
#### Mild leakage in training-season M1

When generating $M_1$ predictions for training season $s$, the reference curve
$g_\text{ref}^{(-s^*)}$ **includes** season $s$ itself (only $s^*$ is excluded).
This means $M_1$'s predictions for training seasons are slightly optimistic
compared to true leave-one-out predictions. This leakage is mild because:

- The reference curve is an average over $\sim$10 seasons — adding or removing
  one season changes it only slightly.
- The test season $s^*$ (which matters for evaluation) always uses a reference
  curve from which it is properly excluded.
- Full nested LOSO (excluding each training season from its own reference curve)
  would require $O(S^2)$ reference curve fits per fold — prohibitive for tuning.
:::

### Fallback to static template

When $M_1$ predictions are unavailable for a row (e.g., pre-ignition weeks
included in the training buffer via $Kb$), $M_2$ falls back to the static
template:

$$
\texttt{logit\_f\_eff}_{s,w,h} =
\begin{cases}
\mathrm{logit}_\varepsilon\!\bigl(\hat{p}^{M_1}_{s,w,h}\bigr) &
  \text{if } \hat{p}^{M_1}_{s,w,h} \text{ is available} \\[6pt]
\omega(t;\,Kr)\;\mathrm{logit}_\varepsilon\!\bigl(g_\text{ref}(w^a + \delta)\bigr) &
  \text{otherwise (original static template)}
\end{cases}
$$


## Deployment: weekly M0 → M1 → M2 chain {#sec-deploy}

At runtime during the live season $s^*$, the weekly cycle becomes:

### Step 1: $M_0$ — Ignition detection

$$
P(s^*,w) \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{run\_ignition\_weekly}(\text{data}_{s^*,1:w},\; \hat\theta).
$$

If $P(s^*,w) = 0$, stop — no forecast this week.

### Step 2: $M_1$ — Alignment

$$
\bigl(\tau_w,\, \delta_w,\, a_w,\, b_w,\, \text{state},\, \text{forecast\_df}\bigr)
\;\leftarrow\; \texttt{run\_alignment\_prospective}(\text{data}_{s^*,1:w},\; g_\text{ref},\; \text{hyper}).
$$

$M_1$'s `forecast_df` contains the predicted curve $\hat\pi^{M_1}_{s^*,w}(t)$
on a dense grid of aligned time $t$.

### Step 3: Inject M1 into M2 snapshots

For each forecast horizon $h \in \{1, 2\}$, interpolate $M_1$'s prediction at
the target week and replace the template feature:

$$
\texttt{logit\_f\_eff}^h_{s^*,w} \;\leftarrow\; \mathrm{logit}_\varepsilon\!\Bigl(\hat\pi^{M_1}_{s^*,w}\!\bigl(w^a_{s^*,w+h}\bigr)\Bigr).
$$

This is performed by `inject_m1_into_snapshots()`, which modifies the M2
prediction snapshots in-place.

### Step 4: $M_2$ — Forecast

$$
\hat\pi^h_{s^*,w} \;\leftarrow\; \texttt{stage2\_predict\_series}(\text{snapshots with } \tilde{z}^h,\; \text{M}_2\text{ fit}).
$$

The full chain is wrapped in `run_m0_m1_m2_weekly()`:

```
Week w arrives
  └─ M₀:  run_ignition_weekly(data[1:w], params)
       └─ if ignited:
            M₁:  run_alignment_prospective(data[1:w], ref, hyper)
                 └─ forecast_df  →  interpolate at target weeks
                      └─ inject_m1_into_snapshots(pp, m1_result, ref)
                           └─ M₂:  stage2_predict_series(pp, stage2_fit)
                                    └─ π̂_{s*,w+1},  π̂_{s*,w+2}
```


## Impact on existing components

### What changes

| Component | Impact |
|---|---|
| **$M_2$ template feature** | `logit_f_eff` now computed from $M_1$'s prediction instead of static $g_\text{ref}(w^a + \delta)$ |
| **$M_2$ hyperparameters** | $\delta$ (template shift) is **eliminated** — $M_1$ handles alignment |
| **$M_2$ LOSO** | Now requires $M_1$ walk-forward per fold; use `loso_m1_m2_joint()` instead of `tune_stage2_loso_spec_grid_parallel()` |
| **Deployment pipeline** | Use `run_m0_m1_m2_weekly()` for full chain |

### What does NOT change

| Component | Status |
|---|---|
| **$M_0$ ignition detection** | Unchanged — still gates everything |
| **$M_1$ alignment model** | Unchanged — same 4-parameter dilation |
| **$M_1$ LOSO** ([`loso_walkforward.html`](loso_walkforward.html)) | Unchanged — still valid for `k_ref` tuning and peak detection |
| **Reference curve estimation** | Unchanged — `estimateRef()` is still the foundation |
| **$M_2$ model structure** | All other terms ($b_s$, $S_2^h$, $S_4^h$, $S_6^h$, $S_7^h$) unchanged |
| **$M_2$ hyperparameters** | $Kr$, $\alpha_\text{state}$, $\lambda_w$, $k_*$ all still tunable |
| **$M_2$ weekly refit** | Same mechanism — only template source differs |


## Key functions {#sec-functions}

All new functions live in `R/m1_m2_bridge.R`:

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `m1_walkforward_predictions(seasonD, ...)` | Run $M_1$ walk-forward for one season, return predictions at target weeks as a data frame with columns `(season, eval_weekF, h, m1_p_hat)` |
| `m1_walkforward_multi(allD, ...)` | Parallelised wrapper — runs `m1_walkforward_predictions` across multiple seasons using `future`/`furrr` |
| `inject_m1_into_snapshots(pp, m1_result, ref)` | Replace `logit_f_eff` in $M_2$ prediction snapshots with $M_1$'s interpolated prediction |
| `run_m0_m1_m2_weekly(currentSeason, ref, hyper, stage2_fit, ...)` | Full $M_0 \to M_1 \to M_2$ weekly chain for deployment |
| `loso_m1_m2_joint(allD, spec, ...)` | Joint $M_0 \to M_1 \to M_2$ LOSO evaluator |

Modified functions in `R/prospective_training.R`:

| Function | Change |
|---|---|
| `prep_stage2_joint(..., m1_preds = NULL)` | When `m1_preds` is provided, joins on `(season, weekF, lead)` and sets `logit_f_eff = logit(m1_p_hat)`, falling back to static template for missing rows |
| `train_stage2_joint(..., m1_preds = NULL)` | Passes `m1_preds` through to `prep_stage2_joint()` |


## Updated training & deployment sequence

Compare with the [pipeline overview](pipeline_overview.html) §7:

| Step | Component | When | Change from original |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reference curve + `hyper` | Offline | *No change* |
| 2 | Ignition detector $\hat\theta$ | Offline | *No change* |
| 3 | $M_1$ LOSO walk-forward → `params_df` | Offline | *No change* (still needed for `k_ref` tuning) |
| 4 | Peak detection tuning | Offline | *No change* |
| **5** | **$M_2$ hyperparameter tuning** | **Offline** | **Use `loso_m1_m2_joint()` — runs $M_1$ walk-forward per fold to generate template features; $\delta$ grid no longer needed** |
| **6** | **$M_2$ initial fit** | **Offline** | **`train_stage2_joint(m1_preds = m1_train_all)` — uses walk-forward $M_1$ predictions on all historical seasons** |
| **7** | **Weekly: $M_0 \to M_1 \to M_2$** | **Online** | **Use `run_m0_m1_m2_weekly()` — $M_1$ output feeds $M_2$'s template via `inject_m1_into_snapshots()`** |
